summary: Facial recognition technology has reached alarming accuracy levels, with a Russian company achieving 73% match rates against million-image databases. The company's founders dismiss privacy concerns with cavalier arguments about progress being inevitable and privacy already being dead. O'Neil contrasts their dismissive attitude with concrete harms: the technology is being sold to authoritarian regimes, deployed for mass surveillance in Moscow, and has already been weaponized to harass women. The post highlights the dangerous gap between rapidly advancing surveillance capabilities and any meaningful ethical oversight.
key_points: NTechLab's facial recognition correctly matches 73% of people against a database of one million photos
The founders dismiss privacy concerns with fatalistic arguments: 'There is no private life' and 'progress wins'
The technology is being negotiated for sale to state security firms in China and Turkey
Moscow plans to install the software on citywide security cameras
The software was already used to identify and harass women alleged to have appeared in pornographic films
money_quotes: There's reason to believe facial recognition software is getting very accurate.
The company, headed by two 20-something Russian tech dudes, are not worried about the ethics of their algorithms.
In the words of one of the founders, 'There is no private life.'
'There is always a conflict between progress and some scared people,' he said. 'But in any way, progress wins.'
They were already involved in a scandal in which people used their software to identify and harass women who had allegedly acted in pornographic films online in Russia.
key_insight: When tech founders dismiss ethics with fatalism about progress, look at who's buying their products and who's already been harmed.
